note_data.title: Analytical Chemistry
keyPoints: **Mass spectrometry** produces a molecular ion peak at m/z equal to the relative molecular mass of the compound
**Fragmentation patterns** in mass spectra show characteristic peaks that help identify functional groups and molecular structure
**Infrared spectroscopy** identifies functional groups by measuring absorption of IR radiation at specific wavenumbers
**1H NMR** shows the number of different hydrogen environments and their relative positions using chemical shift values
**Integration ratios** in NMR spectra give the relative number of hydrogen atoms in each environment
**Coupling patterns** in NMR show splitting due to neighboring hydrogen atoms following the n+1 rule
**Thin layer chromatography** separates compounds based on different affinities for stationary and mobile phases
**Rf values** are calculated as distance moved by compound divided by distance moved by solvent front
**Gas chromatography** separates volatile compounds with retention time identifying specific substances

definitions.definition: The peak in a mass spectrum corresponding to the molecular ion formed by removal of one electron from the molecule
The position of an NMR signal relative to a reference compound, measured in ppm
The time taken for a component to pass through a chromatography column from injection to detection
The ratio of the distance moved by a substance to the distance moved by the solvent front in chromatography

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use data provided to give a logical answer with reasoning
Use all given information to reach a conclusion, showing your working

modelAnswer.question: A compound has the molecular formula C4H8O. Its IR spectrum shows a strong absorption at 1740 cm⁻¹ and its ¹H NMR spectrum shows signals at δ 1.3 (3H, triplet) and δ 4.1 (2H, quartet). Deduce the structure of this compound. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: The IR absorption at 1740 cm⁻¹ indicates a C=O group characteristic of an ester or aldehyde. The NMR shows an ethyl group (CH₃CH₂-) with the triplet at δ 1.3 for CH₃ and quartet at δ 4.1 for CH₂. The chemical shift at δ 4.1 indicates CH₂ attached to oxygen. The structure is ethyl ethanoate, CH₃COOCH₂CH₃.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Identifies C=O group from IR at 1740 cm⁻¹
Mark 2: Identifies ethyl group from NMR coupling pattern
Mark 3: Deduces correct structure as ethyl ethanoate

commonMistakes: Never confuse molecular ion peak with base peak - molecular ion is always at highest m/z value
Students often forget that OH groups in IR spectra show broad absorptions around 3200-3600 cm⁻¹
Common error: not recognizing that integration ratios must be simplified to whole numbers
Students frequently mix up chemical shift ranges - aldehydes are around δ 9-10, not δ 2-3
Never say retention time identifies the compound absolutely - say it helps identify by comparison with known standards
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 4 and Paper 5 - master the characteristic IR frequencies
Always state the evidence from each spectrum before deducing the structure
In chromatography questions, calculate Rf values to 2 decimal places
Learn the chemical shift ranges: alkyl δ 0.8-1.8, C-O δ 3-5, aromatic δ 7-8, aldehyde δ 9-10
formulas: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Mass spectrum showing molecular ion peak and fragmentation pattern with m/z values on x-axis and relative abundance on y-axis
